title_en: Introduction to al-Umm and Imam al-Shafi'i's Methodology
title_ar: مقدمة في كتاب الأم ومنهج الإمام الشافعي
content_en:
Imam Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i was born in Gaza in 150 AH (767 CE), the same year that Imam Abu Hanifah passed away. Raised in Makkah and later traveling to Madinah, he studied under Imam Malik ibn Anas and absorbed the tradition of the people of Madinah deeply. He then traveled to Iraq, where he encountered the Hanafi school and debated its foremost scholars. This exposure to two of the most influential juristic traditions of his time shaped his unique methodology. He eventually settled in Egypt, where his final and most refined opinions — known as his jadid (new) school — were recorded and transmitted.

Al-Umm is the most comprehensive work attributed to al-Shafi'i and stands as the principal reference for the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence. The title means 'The Mother,' signifying its foundational and encompassing nature. It was largely compiled and recorded by his student al-Rabi' ibn Sulayman al-Muradi, who organized his teacher's dictations and discussions into a structured text. The work covers virtually every major topic of Islamic law, from ritual purity to trade, marriage, inheritance, and criminal punishments.

The cornerstone of al-Shafi'i's juristic methodology is his foundational treatise al-Risalah, the first systematic work on the principles of Islamic jurisprudence (usul al-fiqh). In it, he established a strict hierarchy of legal sources. The Quran is the supreme source, binding on all Muslims without qualification. The authenticated Sunnah of the Prophet, upon him be peace, comes immediately after, and al-Shafi'i was among the first to insist that isolated (ahad) hadiths are legally binding as long as their chain of transmission is sound, even when they contradict the practice of a particular city or the opinion of a scholar.

The third source is ijma, the consensus of scholars. Al-Shafi'i accepted consensus as binding but was cautious about claiming it existed on disputed questions, restricting its scope largely to matters all Muslims agreed upon from generation to generation. The fourth source is qiyas, analogical reasoning, which allowed jurists to extend established rulings to new situations sharing the same effective cause. Al-Shafi'i formalized the conditions under which qiyas could be validly applied, making it a disciplined tool rather than an arbitrary exercise of opinion.

One of al-Shafi'i's most significant contributions was his critique of the concept of istihsan (juristic preference), which Hanafi jurists used to depart from strict analogical reasoning when its outcome seemed harsh or contrary to the spirit of the law. Al-Shafi'i argued that istihsan amounted to legislating without textual authority, a charge his opponents disputed vigorously. This debate crystallized one of the enduring methodological differences between the Hanafi and Shafi'i schools.

Within al-Umm, al-Shafi'i frequently engages with the positions of Imam Malik and the Iraqi scholars, explaining why he agrees or disagrees and grounding every conclusion in textual evidence. This transparent argumentation gives the book an almost dialogic character, making it valuable not only as a legal reference but also as a model of scholarly discourse. For students of comparative Islamic jurisprudence, al-Umm remains indispensable as a window into the mind of one of the most consequential jurists in Islamic history.
